hook.text: Your phone was designed in California, assembled in China, and uses minerals from Congo. That's globalization.

buildup.text: Globalization is the growing interconnection of economies, cultures, and societies across national borders.

discovery.text: It accelerated after 1945 with trade agreements, air travel, and the internet shrinking time and distance.

twist.text: Globalization isn't new. The Silk Road connected civilizations centuries ago. The scale and speed are what changed.

climax.text: Globalization links everyone, but not equally. Who benefits and who suffers depends on power and position.

punchline.text: Connected doesn't mean equal.

quiz.question: What is globalization?
options: The growing interconnection of economies, cultures, and societies

hook.text: Is globalization a force for freedom or a tool of domination? Sociologists have sharply different answers.

buildup.text: Hyperglobalists say nation-states are fading as global markets and institutions take over governance.

discovery.text: Skeptics argue globalization is overstated. Nations still control borders, armies, and most economic policy.

twist.text: Transformationalists take the middle ground: globalization is real but uneven, contested, and reversible.

climax.text: How you theorize globalization shapes what solutions you propose for inequality, conflict, and climate change.

punchline.text: Your theory decides your solution.

quiz.question: What do transformationalists argue about globalization?
options: It's real but uneven, contested, and reversible

hook.text: A letter once took months to cross the ocean. Now a video call connects continents in milliseconds.

buildup.text: David Harvey coined time-space compression: technology shrinks the effective distance between places and people.

discovery.text: Jet travel, internet, and phones make global communication instant — collapsing barriers that shaped history.

twist.text: Compression isn't equal. Rural villages may lack internet while urban elites video-call across hemispheres.

climax.text: Time-space compression reorganizes social life. Where you are matters less; what you can access matters more.

punchline.text: Distance shrank, but not for everyone.

quiz.question: What is time-space compression?
options: Technology shrinking effective distance between places

hook.text: Manuel Castells said power no longer sits in buildings. It flows through networks — digital, financial, social.

buildup.text: The network society is organized around information technology networks that connect people, firms, and states.

discovery.text: Networks are flexible and fast. They outperform rigid hierarchies, reshaping how work, politics, and culture operate.

twist.text: Being excluded from networks is the new inequality. Those outside the network are invisible to the global system.

climax.text: Power in the network society belongs to those who control flows of information, capital, and connection.

punchline.text: Disconnection is the new poverty.

quiz.question: What is Castells' network society?
options: Society organized around information technology networks

hook.text: A virus in Wuhan shut down the entire planet. Globalization connected us — and made us all vulnerable.

buildup.text: Ulrich Beck described the risk society: modern life creates global risks that no single nation can control.

discovery.text: Climate change, pandemics, nuclear threats, and financial crises are manufactured risks with global reach.

twist.text: Risks are democratic in reach but not in impact. The poor suffer most from crises they didn't create.

climax.text: Globalization binds humanity together in shared risk. Solving these threats requires cooperation beyond borders.

punchline.text: Global problems need global solutions.

quiz.question: What is Beck's 'risk society'?
options: Modern life creates global risks no single nation can control

hook.text: Money never sleeps. When Wall Street closes, Tokyo opens. Capital circles the globe twenty-four hours a day.

buildup.text: Global capitalism is an economic system where production, finance, and trade operate across national borders.

discovery.text: Wallerstein's world-systems theory divides nations into core, periphery, and semi-periphery based on economic role.

twist.text: Core nations extract wealth from periphery nations through unequal trade, debt, and labor exploitation.

climax.text: Global capitalism generates enormous wealth — but distributes it along lines of power established by colonialism.

punchline.text: The global economy has winners by design.

quiz.question: What does Wallerstein's world-systems theory describe?
options: Nations divided into core, periphery, and semi-periphery by economic role

hook.text: Apple's revenue exceeds the GDP of most countries. Some corporations are more powerful than many nations.

buildup.text: Multinational corporations operate across borders, moving production where labor is cheapest and taxes lowest.

discovery.text: They create jobs in developing nations but often under poor conditions with wages that keep workers in poverty.

twist.text: MNCs can pressure governments to lower regulations. The threat of leaving gives corporations political leverage.

climax.text: Multinationals drive economic growth globally but concentrate power in ways that challenge democratic governance.

punchline.text: Corporations outgrew the nations that made them.

quiz.question: How do multinational corporations challenge governance?
options: They can pressure governments by threatening to relocate

hook.text: Your $5 t-shirt was sewn by someone earning $2 a day. Global supply chains hide that human cost.

buildup.text: Global labor markets let companies outsource production to countries with the lowest wages and weakest protections.

discovery.text: Sweatshops, child labor, and unsafe conditions persist because global competition drives a race to the bottom.

twist.text: Workers organize across borders too. Global labor movements push for fair wages and safety standards worldwide.

climax.text: Cheap goods have a hidden price: human exploitation embedded in every link of the global supply chain.

punchline.text: Someone pays for every bargain you find.

quiz.question: What does the 'race to the bottom' mean?
options: Nations lowering wages and protections to attract business

hook.text: Free markets, low taxes, privatization, deregulation. These ideas reshaped the world — and not equally.

buildup.text: Neoliberalism promotes free markets, minimal state intervention, and private enterprise as the path to prosperity.

discovery.text: Since the 1980s, neoliberal policies spread globally through institutions like the IMF, World Bank, and WTO.

twist.text: Critics argue neoliberalism widened inequality, weakened public services, and shifted risk from states to individuals.

climax.text: Neoliberalism isn't just economics. It's an ideology that turns citizens into consumers and society into a market.

punchline.text: The market became the answer to everything.

quiz.question: What does neoliberalism promote?
options: Free markets, privatization, and minimal state intervention

hook.text: In 2008, American banks collapsed and Greek workers lost pensions. Financial contagion respects no borders.

buildup.text: Global financial integration means that a crisis in one country can cascade across the entire world economy.

discovery.text: Deregulation allowed risky lending and speculation. When the bubble burst, governments bailed out banks, not people.

twist.text: Austerity measures punished ordinary citizens for crises created by financial elites. The pain was not shared equally.

climax.text: Global finance connects prosperity and disaster. Economic interdependence means shared risk without shared control.

punchline.text: The gains are private; the losses are public.

quiz.question: Why do financial crises spread globally?
options: Financial integration means crises cascade across borders

hook.text: The United Nations has 193 member states. It can debate anything. It can enforce almost nothing.

buildup.text: International organizations like the UN, WHO, and WTO govern issues that cross borders: trade, health, security.

discovery.text: They set rules, provide aid, and mediate conflicts. But they depend on powerful states for funding and enforcement.

twist.text: Critics say these bodies serve wealthy nations' interests. The Security Council's veto power proves the point.

climax.text: International organizations represent global governance's promise and its limits — cooperation shaped by power.

punchline.text: Global rules, unequal rule-makers.

quiz.question: What limits international organizations' effectiveness?
options: They depend on powerful states for funding and enforcement

hook.text: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights promised dignity for all in 1948. Millions still wait.

buildup.text: Human rights frameworks assert that all people deserve basic protections regardless of nationality or culture.

discovery.text: International courts, treaties, and NGOs enforce human rights, but enforcement remains weak and selective.

twist.text: Powerful nations violate rights with impunity. Weak nations face sanctions. Justice follows the global power map.

climax.text: Global justice is an aspiration, not a reality. Its promise matters even when its practice falls short.

punchline.text: Rights exist on paper; power decides in practice.

quiz.question: What limits global human rights enforcement?
options: Powerful nations can violate rights with less consequence
All nations are held equally accountable

hook.text: A country signs a trade deal and can no longer set its own tariffs. Did it just lose its sovereignty?

buildup.text: Sovereignty is a nation's right to govern itself. Globalization challenges this through treaties and institutions.

discovery.text: EU members share currency and regulations. WTO rules limit trade policy. Nations trade autonomy for access.

twist.text: Brexit showed sovereignty's appeal. Millions voted to leave the EU to reclaim national control — at economic cost.

climax.text: Globalization doesn't erase sovereignty — it transforms it. Nations choose which powers to share and which to keep.

punchline.text: Independence now comes with conditions.

quiz.question: How does globalization affect sovereignty?
options: Nations trade some autonomy for access to global systems

hook.text: Climate strikes happened in 150 countries on the same day. Protest went global like the problems it fights.

buildup.text: Global social movements organize across borders to address issues no single nation can solve alone.

discovery.text: Digital tools enable coordination: activists share strategies, build solidarity, and mobilize millions online.

twist.text: Global movements face challenges: cultural gaps, language barriers, and the distance between online activism and change.

climax.text: Global movements show that civil society can challenge power across borders — even without formal authority.

punchline.text: People organized before governments did.

quiz.question: What enables global social movements?
options: Digital tools that allow cross-border coordination

hook.text: Globalization was supposed to make nations obsolete. Instead, nationalism surged worldwide. Nobody predicted that.

buildup.text: Nationalism is loyalty to one's nation and the belief that national interests should come first.

discovery.text: Globalization's disruptions — job losses, cultural change, inequality — fuel nationalist backlash and populist leaders.

twist.text: Nationalist leaders use global media to spread their anti-global message. The tools of globalization serve its critics.

climax.text: Nationalism isn't globalization's opposite. It's a reaction to it — born from the anxieties that connection creates.

punchline.text: Globalization created its own backlash.

quiz.question: Why has nationalism surged alongside globalization?

hook.text: 281 million people live outside their birth country. That's more than the entire population of most nations.

buildup.text: Migration is driven by push factors like poverty and conflict, and pull factors like jobs and safety.

discovery.text: Most migration is regional, not intercontinental. Africans move within Africa; Asians move within Asia.

twist.text: Migrants send $600 billion home yearly in remittances — more than all foreign aid combined. Migration builds economies.

climax.text: Migration is humanity's oldest response to hardship. Borders are new; human movement is not.

punchline.text: People have always moved toward hope.

quiz.question: What are 'push factors' in migration?
options: Reasons people are attracted to a destination
Conditions like poverty and conflict that drive people to leave

hook.text: Chinese New Year is celebrated in 120 countries. The diaspora carried culture across every ocean.

buildup.text: A diaspora is a community of people who share a homeland but live scattered across multiple countries.

discovery.text: Diasporas maintain cultural ties through food, language, religion, media, and regular contact with the homeland.

twist.text: Modern technology strengthens diaspora bonds. Video calls and social media keep transnational families connected daily.

climax.text: Diasporas challenge the idea that identity is tied to place. Belonging can span continents and generations.

punchline.text: Home is a feeling, not just a place.

quiz.question: What defines a diaspora community?
options: People sharing a homeland but living scattered across countries

hook.text: 100 million people are forcibly displaced worldwide. That's more than the population of most European countries.

buildup.text: Refugees flee persecution, war, and violence. Internally displaced people flee but remain within their own country.

discovery.text: The 1951 Refugee Convention guarantees protection, but many nations restrict entry and detain asylum seekers.

twist.text: Most refugees are hosted by developing nations, not wealthy ones. The poorest countries carry the heaviest burden.

climax.text: Forced migration reveals global inequality at its starkest: the powerful create crises; the powerless flee them.

punchline.text: Nobody chooses to be a refugee.

quiz.question: Which countries host the most refugees?

hook.text: She speaks Mandarin at home, English at work, and Spanish with friends. Which culture is hers? All of them.

buildup.text: Transnational identity means belonging to multiple cultures and nations simultaneously, without choosing just one.

discovery.text: Globalization creates hybrid identities: people blend languages, foods, values, and traditions from multiple worlds.

twist.text: Traditional sociology assumed one identity per person. Transnational lives expose that assumption as outdated.

climax.text: Identity in a global world isn't a single story. It's a mosaic assembled from every place you've called home.

punchline.text: Identity doesn't fit inside one border.

quiz.question: What is transnational identity?
options: Belonging to multiple cultures and nations simultaneously

hook.text: Build the wall. Close the borders. Take our country back. Fear of migration reshapes politics worldwide.

buildup.text: Anti-immigration movements argue that migration threatens national identity, security, jobs, and social cohesion.

discovery.text: Research consistently shows immigrants contribute more in taxes than they consume in services in most countries.

twist.text: Anti-immigration sentiment often rises during economic downturns, when people seek scapegoats for structural problems.

climax.text: Anti-immigration politics reveals how economic anxiety gets redirected into cultural fear and xenophobia.

punchline.text: Blame the newcomer, not the system.

quiz.question: When does anti-immigration sentiment typically rise?
options: During economic downturns when people seek scapegoats

hook.text: The richest 1% own more wealth than the bottom 50% combined. Global inequality is staggering by any measure.

buildup.text: Global inequality is measured by income, wealth, health, education, and life expectancy across and within nations.

discovery.text: The Gini coefficient ranks countries by inequality. The Human Development Index measures quality of life more broadly.

twist.text: Between-country inequality narrowed as China and India grew. Within-country inequality has widened almost everywhere.

climax.text: How you measure inequality shapes what you see. Every metric reveals a different piece of the global puzzle.

punchline.text: Numbers only capture what you choose to count.

quiz.question: What does the Gini coefficient measure?
options: Income or wealth inequality within a country

hook.text: Colonialism officially ended decades ago. Its economic and political scars shape global inequality to this day.

buildup.text: European powers extracted resources, labor, and wealth from colonies for centuries, enriching the colonizers.

discovery.text: Borders drawn by colonizers ignored ethnic and cultural groups, creating conflicts that persist in Africa and Asia.

twist.text: Neocolonialism continues through debt, trade rules, and foreign intervention that maintain old power structures.

climax.text: Global inequality didn't happen naturally. It was built through centuries of extraction that still shapes the map.

punchline.text: The past is still shaping the present.

quiz.question: What is neocolonialism?
options: Continued control through debt, trade rules, and intervention

hook.text: Trillions in aid have flowed to developing nations. Poverty persists. Something in the model isn't working.

buildup.text: Development theory evolved from modernization — follow the West — to dependency theory — the West holds you back.

discovery.text: Foreign aid can help, but it often comes with strings: policy conditions that serve donors more than recipients.

twist.text: Some argue aid creates dependency. Others say the real problem is that aid is too small and too conditional.

climax.text: Development isn't about charity. It's about restructuring global systems that produce inequality in the first place.

punchline.text: Aid can't fix what trade creates.

quiz.question: What does dependency theory argue?
options: Wealthy nations' dominance holds poorer nations back

hook.text: Life expectancy in Japan is 84. In Sierra Leone, it's 54. Where you're born decides how long you live.

buildup.text: Global health inequality reflects differences in wealth, infrastructure, nutrition, and access to medical care.

discovery.text: Preventable diseases still kill millions in poor nations while wealthy nations spend billions on cosmetic procedures.

twist.text: COVID-19 exposed health inequality globally. Wealthy nations hoarded vaccines while poorer nations waited in line.

climax.text: Health inequality isn't about biology. It's about global structures that distribute resources based on wealth, not need.

punchline.text: Your zip code predicts your health more than your genes.

quiz.question: What primarily drives global health inequality?
options: Differences in wealth, infrastructure, and access to care

hook.text: Women do 75% of unpaid work globally. They earn less, own less, and hold fewer seats of power everywhere.

buildup.text: Gender inequality intersects with global inequality: women in poor nations face compounded disadvantages.

discovery.text: Girls' education is the strongest development tool known. Educated women have fewer children and earn more.

twist.text: Globalization brings progress and exploitation. Factory jobs empower some women while trapping others in sweatshops.

climax.text: Gender equality isn't just a women's issue. It's a development issue that shapes outcomes for entire societies.

punchline.text: Empower women and nations rise with them.

quiz.question: Why is girls' education considered a key development tool?
options: Educated women earn more and have fewer children, improving outcomes

hook.text: The nations that pollute the least suffer the most from climate change. Injustice is baked into the atmosphere.

buildup.text: Climate change is a social crisis as much as an environmental one. It deepens inequality between and within nations.

discovery.text: Wealthy nations industrialized using fossil fuels. Now they ask developing nations to skip that path without aid.

twist.text: Climate refugees are already moving. Rising seas, droughts, and storms displace millions with no legal protection.

climax.text: Climate change is the ultimate global problem: caused collectively, suffered unequally, and solvable only together.

punchline.text: The biggest polluters feel the least pain.

quiz.question: Why is climate change a social justice issue?
options: The nations that pollute least suffer the most

hook.text: COVID-19 killed millions. But it didn't kill equally. Class, race, and nation determined who survived.

buildup.text: Pandemics expose and deepen existing inequalities in healthcare, housing, employment, and social protection.

discovery.text: Essential workers — often low-paid and from minority groups — faced the highest exposure and the least protection.

twist.text: Vaccine nationalism meant wealthy nations secured doses first. Global cooperation collapsed when it mattered most.

climax.text: Pandemics don't create inequality. They reveal it — stripping away the illusion that everyone faces the same risks.

punchline.text: The virus was equal; the response wasn't.

quiz.question: How did COVID-19 reveal social inequality?
options: Class, race, and nation determined exposure, treatment, and survival

hook.text: China's social credit system scores citizens on behavior. Dystopian? Many democracies track citizens too.

buildup.text: Governments worldwide use digital surveillance for security, from facial recognition to mass data collection.

discovery.text: Surveillance technologies developed in democracies are exported to authoritarian regimes that use them for repression.

twist.text: The line between safety and control blurs. Anti-terror tools monitor activists, journalists, and ordinary citizens.

climax.text: Global surveillance raises a fundamental question: can freedom survive when every action is watched and recorded?

punchline.text: Security and freedom fight for the same space.

quiz.question: What concern do surveillance technologies raise?
options: They blur the line between security and repressive control

hook.text: The world produces enough food for 10 billion people. Yet 800 million go hungry. This is a distribution problem.

buildup.text: Food insecurity is the lack of reliable access to affordable, nutritious food — a crisis of systems, not supply.

discovery.text: Global agriculture is dominated by multinational corporations that prioritize profit over local food needs.

twist.text: Climate change threatens harvests globally. The poorest farmers face the worst impacts with the fewest resources.

climax.text: Hunger is not caused by scarcity. It's caused by inequality — who grows, who owns, and who eats are all political.

punchline.text: Hunger is a choice — society's, not nature's.

quiz.question: Why does hunger persist despite sufficient global food production?

hook.text: Pandemics, wars, and nationalism test globalization. Is the world pulling apart or finding new ways to connect?

buildup.text: Some scholars predict deglobalization as nations prioritize self-sufficiency and shorten supply chains.

discovery.text: Others see reglobalization: new connections forming around digital economies, green energy, and regional blocs.

twist.text: Technology ensures global connection will continue. The question isn't whether but how and for whose benefit.

climax.text: The future of globalization depends on choices: will it serve markets alone, or build a more just world?

punchline.text: The world stays connected; the terms are negotiable.

quiz.question: What is the key question about globalization's future?
options: Whether it will serve markets alone or build a more just world
